case.title: A phototoxic drug reaction due to topical NSAIDs
case.case_prompt: A 52â€yearâ€old man with Addisonâ€™s disease on lifelong corticosteroid replacement and a history of lateral epicondylitis presented with a 7â€day history of severe redness around his right elbow accompanied by intense burning and stinging. The redness began after he had been gardening on a cloudy summer day. Over the next days, his elbow became swollen, blisters formed and then ruptured, leaving crusted lesions. His general practitioner suspected cellulitis and prescribed dicloxacillin. Two days after starting antibiotics, he developed an itchy rash on his chest and abdomen. On examination, there was a bright red, edematous, crusted erythema over the right elbow and a maculopapular rash on the trunk. Laboratory studies, including Câ€reactive protein and complete blood count, were within normal limits.

Nonspecific dermatitis considered but exposure pattern pointed toward a photoreaction â€” â€œDifferential diagnosis for phototoxic reactions should include all types of nonspecific dermatitis. Ways of approaching the diagnosis include noticing the distribution of the lesions, in that skin under the chin, nose, and other areas may be lesionâ€free due to protection from direct sun exposure.â€

case.title: Recurrent Ameloblastic Fibroma: Report of a Rare Case
case.case_prompt:
An 18-year-old woman presented with a 1-year history of slowly enlarging gingival overgrowth in the left posterior mandible that interfered with chewing but was painless. Intraoral examination revealed a 3Ã—4 cm exophytic mass extending from the left mandibular second molar to the retromolar pad, buccally into the vestibule and inferiorly to the floor of the mouth. Panoramic radiograph showed a well-defined radiolucency around the impacted left third molar. The lesion and the impacted tooth were excised en bloc. 

Grossly, the specimen included both intraosseous and extraosseous components. Histologic examination demonstrated cords, interconnecting strands, and islands of odontogenic epithelium embedded in a cell-rich, myxoid mesenchymal stroma. The epithelial strands and cords were lined by a double layer of cuboidal cells. The islands exhibited peripheral tall columnar cells with polarized nuclei and clear, vacuolated cytoplasm surrounding central stellate reticulum-like cells. Juxtaepithelial hyalinization was noted around some islands. No hard-tissue (enamel or dentin) formation was seen. The cellularity varied, with focal hypercellular areas and other sparsely cellular, myxoid regions. A thin fibrous capsule partially surrounded the lesion. No cytologic atypia or mitotic figures were observed on multiple sections.
case.diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Ameloblastoma â€” â€œAF needs to be differentiated from ameloblastomaâ€¦â€ 2. Odontogenic myxoma â€” â€œAF needs to be differentiated from â€¦ odontogenic myxomaâ€¦â€ 3. Dentigerous cyst â€” â€œAF needs to be differentiated from â€¦ dentigerous cystâ€¦â€ 4. Odontogenic keratocyst â€” â€œAF needs to be differentiated from â€¦ odontogenic keratocystâ€¦â€ 5. Central giant cell granuloma â€” â€œAF needs to be differentiated from â€¦ central giant cell granulomaâ€¦â€ 6. Histiocytosis â€” â€œAF needs to be differentiated from â€¦ histiocytosis.â€ 7. Ameloblastic fibroodontoma/fibrodentinoma â€” â€œThere was no hard tissue formation, thus eliminating ameloblastic fibroodontoma/fibrodentinoma.â€ 8. Ameloblastic fibrosarcoma â€” â€œPresence of numerous mitotic cells or any atypical mitosis should suggest malignant entities such as AFS in the differential diagnosisâ€¦ atypia and mitotic activities were not evident.â€

case.title:
Refractory scleroderma renal crisis precipitated after high-dose oral
corticosteroids and concurrent intravitreal injection of
bevacizumab
case.case_prompt:
A 37-year-old man presented with a 3-month history of progressive skin thickening, initially on his torso and then spreading diffusely, accompanied by a 20â€“30-lb weight loss and fatigue. He denied Raynaudâ€™s phenomenon, dyspnea, or wheezing. His blood pressure at presentation was 100â€“110 mmHg systolic, with a serum creatinine of 0.8 mg/dL. He had a history of treated hepatitis B without active disease.

Serologic studies showed a negative antinuclear antibody, negative antiâ€“Smith and antiâ€“ribonucleoprotein antibodies, and low-level antiâ€“topoisomerase I (3â€“4 AU/mL). Nailfold capillaroscopy was suggestive of systemic sclerosis, and a skin biopsy was read as suspicious for morphea versus systemic sclerosis. Echocardiography revealed no pulmonary hypertension or pericardial effusion. He was started on mycophenolate mofetil.

An IgG Î» monoclonal protein of 1.1 g/dL was detected. Bone marrow biopsy showed 10% Î»-restricted plasma cells without high-risk cytogenetics besides 1q and 5q gains, monosomy 13, and 14q deletions. Three months after presentation, for unclear reasons, he was started on high-dose prednisone (60 mg daily). Shortly thereafter, his systolic blood pressure increased to 140â€“150 mmHg and serum creatinine rose to 1.1 mg/dL. He developed blurry vision; ophthalmologic examination revealed cotton-wool spots. He received two intravitreal injections of bevacizumab (1.25 mg each).

One week after the injections, he was admitted with severe hypertension (systolic blood pressures 200â€“220 mmHg), a rise in serum creatinine to 1.4 mg/dL, and new proteinuria (urine protein-creatinine ratio 1 g/g). Renal ultrasound with Doppler showed normal-sized kidneys and no evidence of renal artery stenosis. Given the abrupt hypertension, worsening renal function, proteinuria, recent corticosteroid exposure, and intravitreal VEGF blockade, scleroderma renal crisis was suspected, and a complement-mediated thrombotic microangiopathy related to VEGF inhibition could not be ruled out. A renal biopsy was planned after blood pressure control.
case.diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Scleroderma renal crisis was the leading diagnosis given malignant hypertension and acute kidney injury in a patient with systemic sclerosis after corticosteroid exposure â€” â€œGiven the sudden change in blood pressure, renal function, proteinuria, and recent exposure to oral corticosteroids, SRC was suspected.â€ 2. VEGF-blockadeâ€“induced thrombotic microangiopathy remained in the differential because intravitreal bevacizumab can cause endothelial injury and TMA â€” â€œA complement-mediated TMA related to intravitreal VEGF inhibition could also not be ruled out.â€

case.title: Gallbladder volvulus in a 5â€“years old Vietnamese female: A case report
case.case_prompt:
A previously healthy 5â€yearâ€old girl presented with 9 hours of intermittent, moderateâ€“severity epigastric pain radiating to the right lower quadrant. The pain was unchanged by position and was associated with multiple episodes of nonbloody vomiting. She was afebrile, had normal urination and bowel movements, and reported a similar, selfâ€limited episode 1 month earlier.

On examination, she was alert, without signs of systemic infection. Abdominal palpation elicited tenderness in the epigastrium; there was no guarding or rebound. Murphyâ€™s sign was positive, and there was no jaundice.

Laboratory studies showed normal hepatic and biliary function tests and an elevated C-reactive protein level of 30.2 mg/L. A supine abdominal radiograph was unremarkable.

Initial abdominal ultrasound demonstrated an enlarged gallbladder (54 Ã— 34 mm) with a 3.2 mm wall thickness, pericholecystic fluid, increased pericholecystic fat, and no gallstones or intraluminal nodules. On repeat ultrasound 24 hours later, the gallbladder measured 53 Ã— 33 mm with a 3.1 mm wall, lacked vascular flow, contained biliary sludge, and showed a cone-shaped hypoechoic structure at the neck; the fundus was displaced to the left of its fossa and moved with patient repositioning.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed a 53.5 Ã— 22.8 Ã— 31.5 mm gallbladder with an irregular, poorly enhancing wall, an intraluminal hyperdense area suggestive of hemorrhage, a 3 mm hyperdense nodule, fundus deviation to the left of the gallbladder bed, pericholecystic fluid and fat stranding, and focal hepatic perfusion abnormalities.
case.diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Ileosigmoid knot, pseudo-obstruction, severe constipation, and sigmoid diverticular disease were considered given acute abdominal pain but were excluded because â€œabdominal xâ€ray on admission was normal.â€ 2. Acute cholecystitis was suspected based on gallbladder enlargement, wall thickening, and pericholecystic fluid but lacked gallstones and showed progressive vascular compromise: â€œno stone, and no nodule were the evocative signs of the inflammation of the gallbladder,â€ and later â€œin lack of vascular supply â€¦ suggestive sign of acute GV.â€ 3. case.case_prompt: A 51-year-old woman with Crohnâ€™s disease on infliximab presented with a 2-day history of a bullous rash on her left arm, axilla, and lateral chest wall accompanied by subjective fever. Two days before presentation, she received her second dose of the recombinant adjuvant Shingrix vaccine. She denied new medications or topical products and had no prior similar rashes. Her Crohnâ€™s disease was at baseline with intermittent loose stools. On examination, there was diffuse erythema and swelling from the midchest to the axilla and upper arm, with multiple bullae, some with central dusky areas; mucosal surfaces were spared. case.title: Rapidly progressive central retinal pigmented epithelium atrophy after Ivospemin (SBP-101) treatment for pancreatic adenocarcinoma
case.journal: American Journal of Ophthalmology Case Reports
case.case_prompt: A 69-year-old woman with metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (peritoneal and hepatic metastases) enrolled in a trial of SBP-101 combined with nab-paclitaxel and gemcitabine presented with a 6-month history of progressive blurred vision and visual field loss. She had no diabetes or hypertension. Baseline ophthalmologic evaluation before SBP-101 showed 20/20 visual acuity (VA) in both eyes, normal intraocular pressures, and normal macular structure on optical coherence tomography (OCT). After the second monthly cycle of SBP-101, her VA fell to 20/50 in the right eye and 20/40 in the left eye. Fundus examination revealed pigmentary changes in the macula, with punctate hypo- and hyperautofluorescent spots on blue-autofluorescence. OCT demonstrated ellipsoid zone disruption and hyperreflective material between the Bruch membrane and retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) in both eyes. SBP-101 was discontinued, and intravitreal dexamethasone was administered bilaterally without improvement. One month later, VA was 20/50 OD and 20/32 OS. Full-field electroretinography (ERG) showed normal scotopic responses and photopic amplitudes at the lower limit of normal. Serum anti-recoverin and anti-enolase autoantibodies were within normal limits. Over the next five months, VA declined to 20/5000 OD and 20/4000 OS, with photopsias and severely impaired central vision. Ultraâ€“wide-field fundus photographs revealed bilateral roundish macular areas of irregular pigment loss extending beyond the vascular arcades, with scattered dot-blot hemorrhages. Blue-autofluorescence showed central hypoautofluorescence surrounded by a ring of alternating hyper- and hypoautofluorescent dots. OCT of the macula demonstrated incomplete RPE and outer retinal atrophy with signal hypertransmission to the choroid, ellipsoid zone disruption, and perifoveal hyperreflective sub-RPE deposits; choroidal thickness was reduced. Optic nerve OCT and RNFL thickness were normal. Visual fields showed bilateral central relative scotomas (VFI 26% OD, 31% OS). ERG per ISCEV standards revealed preserved dark-adapted 0.01 cdÂ·s/m2 and mixed rodâ€“cone responses, relatively preserved a-waves on light-adapted single flashes, but reduced amplitudes and slightly prolonged implicit times on 30 Hz flicker.
case.diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Cisplatin-induced toxicity was reviewed since cisplatin can cause cone dysfunction and ischemic retinal changes â€” â€œcisplatin induces a dose-dependent damage photoreceptor cells, presenting in the form of cone malfunction, macular pigmentary changes and minor retinal ischemia changesâ€ â€” yet was deemed unlikely as the patient had not received cisplatin. 2. Cancer-associated retinopathy (CAR) was evaluated because CAR can present with retinal atrophy and ERG abnormalities â€” â€œCAR â€¦ initial phases show a normal fundus â€¦ successively retinal atrophy â€¦ may appearâ€ â€” but was ruled out as â€œscotopic responses [were] within the limits,â€ photopic changes were progressive but localized, and â€œanti-enolase and anti-recoverin [were] â€¦ within the limits.â€

case.title: Seizure and coma secondary to Connâ€™s syndrome: a case report
case.case_prompt: A 48-year-old man of African descent was brought to the emergency department unresponsive after a generalized tonicâ€“clonic seizure. On examination he was comatose with a blood pressure of 178/121 mmHg, heart rate 105 beats/min, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, and a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 3 of 15; he required intubation and ICU admission. He had no history of epilepsy, head trauma, recreational drug use, smoking, or alcohol consumption. His medical history was notable for resistant hypertension diagnosed 20 years ago, treated with atenolol 50 mg, spironolactone 100 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, and valsartan 160 mg daily, with poor compliance, and for type II diabetes mellitus treated with a sulfonylurea. Family history was positive for hypertension but negative for renal disease or adrenal tumors. Body mass index was 31. Thyroid and visual-field examinations were normal, and there were no cushingoid or acromegaly features. Initial laboratory studies showed a potassium level of 2.6 mmol/L (normal 3.6â€“5.1), creatinine 113 Î¼mol/L, metabolic alkalosis on blood gas analysis, urea 5.7 mmol/L, and creatine kinase 2.2 Î¼g/L. Urinalysis was negative for leukocyte esterase and nitrites. A chest radiograph showed no signs of heart failure. ECG demonstrated sinus rhythm. Troponin-T and BNP were within normal limits. Calcium was 2.46 mmol/L. CT of the brain showed no lesions, and MRI the next day revealed no mass-occupying lesion; EEG was normal. Repeat potassium remained low at 2.6 mmol/L; plasma aldosterone was 36.2 ng/dL (normal 2.8â€“15.8), and plasma renin was < 0.081 ng/dL (normal 0.4â€“2.3). A 24-hour urine collection excluded Cushingâ€™s syndrome and pheochromocytoma. Renal ultrasound and cardiac echocardiography excluded renovascular and cardiac causes of secondary hypertension.

Cardiac arrhythmia (atrial fibrillation) ruled out â€” â€œCardiovascular risk factors leading to neurological manifestations, such as atrial fibrillation, were ruled out by a normal ECG.â€ 4. Uremic encephalopathy excluded â€” â€œRenal failure and uremic encephalopathy were excluded by normal kidney function parameters.â€ 5. Renovascular and cardiac causes of secondary hypertension excluded â€” â€œRenal ultrasound and cardiac echography results excluded the presence of renovascular and cardiac causes for secondary hypertension.â€

case.title: Sclerosing meningioma with a large peritumoral cyst: Case report
case.case_prompt: A 53-year-old man with no significant medical history presented with a headache. Neurologic examination revealed that he was alert and had no motor or sensory deficits, but he noted narrowing of the left lower visual field quadrant. Noncontrast CT of the head showed a 5-cm, well-circumscribed, high-density mass in the right parietal region without calcification. MRI demonstrated an extra-axial lesion that was isoâ€intense on T1-weighted imaging, uniformly very low in signal on T2-weighted imaging, and showed marked enhancement after gadolinium administration. There was no diffusion restriction after correction for a T2 blackout effect. The mass was separate from the ventricles and was associated with a large anterior cyst whose contents had a signal different from cerebrospinal fluid; the cyst wall did not enhance, consistent with a peritumoral cyst, and there was surrounding edema. The tumor contacted the dura mater convexity and the falx cerebri but lacked an obvious attachment or dural tail sign. Time-resolved MR angiography (4D-TRAK) showed no tumor staining or feeding vessels, suggesting low vascularity. Routine blood tests, including tumor markers, were unremarkable. Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed no extracranial source of malignancy.
case.diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Metastatic tumor was considered but ruled out â€” â€œcontrastâ€enhanced CT of the trunk showed no mass lesions that could be the primary tumor.â€

case.title: Neglected adult PFAPA syndrome
case.case_prompt:
A 69-year-old woman reported a 5-year history of recurrent febrile episodes every 6â€“8 weeks. Each episode featured a temperature of 38.5â€“40 Â°C lasting up to 7 days, accompanied by sore throat, cervical and submandibular lymphadenopathy and pain, headache, myalgia, fatigue, and dysphagia. She was asymptomatic between episodes and could predict their onset. She had a history of childhood tonsillitis treated with tonsillectomy. She worked in black metal analysis for over 30 years.  

During episodes, laboratory studies showed CRP 64 mg/L, serum amyloid A 140 mg/L, leukocytes 2.94 Ã— 10^9/L, and neutrophils 3.19 Ã— 10^9/L; procalcitonin, immunoglobulins, and ANA titres were normal. HIV, CMV, EBV, and other infection indicators were negative, and blood and throat cultures showed no growth. PET-CT demonstrated increased glucose metabolism in the sigmoid colon, but colonoscopy was normal. Pharyngoscopy revealed pharyngitis with white exudate. Ultrasound showed enlarged bilateral cervical, submandibular, and supraclavicular lymph nodes (up to 21 Ã— 6 mm), and a supraclavicular lymph node biopsy revealed reactive hyperplasia. Bone marrow biopsy and cytogenetics were unremarkable. Despite multiple courses of antibiotics, febrile episodes persisted.
case.diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Infection excluded â€” â€œHIV, CMV, EBV, and other infection indicators were negative. No abnormalities were found in the bacterial cultures of the throat and blood samples.â€ 2. Neoplastic disease excluded â€” â€œPET-CT Indicated increased glucose metabolism in the sigmoid colon â€¦ while the colonoscopy results were normal. â€¦ lymph node biopsy pathology indicated reactive lymph node hyperplasia.â€ 3. Hyper IgD syndrome excluded â€” â€œHigh IgD levels and decreased mevalonate kinase activity support the diagnosis of HIDS.â€ 4. PFAPA syndrome supported â€” â€œPeriodic fever with cervical lymphadenitis and pharyngitis. Inflammatory markers are elevated during fever attacks and without any symptoms during the febrile intervals. Throat swab cultures performed with fever was negative and not responsive to antibiotic therapy. Infectious, neoplastic diseases, monogenic and febrile polygenic auto inflammatory diseases (AIDs) were excluded.â€

case.title: Rhabdomyolysis as cause, consequence, or mimicker of myocardial infarction: A case report
case.case_prompt:
A 65-year-old man was brought to the emergency department because of progressive fatigue, diffuse myalgia, and altered mental status. His medical history included chronic ischemic heart disease status post coronary-artery bypass grafting, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and stage 3 chronic kidney disease.  

On arrival, he reported several days of worsening leg and shoulder pain with an inability to walk independently. Vital signs showed hypotension, and an electrocardiogram revealed atrial fibrillation, a left bundle-branch block, ST-segment depression, and peaked, high T waves. Laboratory tests demonstrated a troponin T level of 532 ng/L (normal < 14 ng/L) and NT-proBNP of 5579 ng/L (normal < 125 ng/L). A temporary pacemaker was placed because of bradyarrhythmia.  

Urgent coronary angiography showed severe three-vessel atheromatous disease with an 80% stenosis in the left anterior descending artery and sub-occlusive lesions in the right coronary artery, which was successfully stented. During the procedure, he developed cardiogenic shock, treated with vasopressors and inotropic support. Arterial blood gas analysis disclosed a metabolic acidosis (pH 7.26) and a serum potassium concentration of 7.5 mmol/L.  

He was admitted to the intensive care unit. Further laboratory results included a creatine phosphokinase level > 22,000 U/L (normal 39â€“308 U/L), a myoglobin level > 30,000 Î¼g/L (normal < 72 Î¼g/L), aspartate aminotransferase 1406 U/L (normal < 50 U/L), lactate dehydrogenase 1969 U/L (normal 50â€“250 U/L), blood urea nitrogen 304 mg/dL (normal 10â€“71 mg/dL), and serum creatinine 6.58 mg/dL (normal 0.67â€“1.17 mg/dL).
case.diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Initial suspicion of myocardial infarction â€” â€œOn arrival â€¦ electrocardiogram revealed â€¦ ST-segment depression and peaked, high T wavesâ€ 2. Hyperkalaemia can mimic ischemic EKG changes â€” â€œpotassium disorders can cause EKG alterations including peaked T waves and bradyarrhythmia, present in our patientâ€™s EKGâ€ 3. MI-associated rhabdomyolysis after defibrillation is unlikely here â€” â€œAssociation between MI and rhabdomyolysis â€¦ usually occurring after cardiac arrest, CPR, and defibrillationâ€

case.title: Ice Pack Test Eased Ptosis in a Patient Presenting with a Possible Oculomotor Nerve Schwannoma: A Case Report
case.case_prompt:
A 32-year-old man presented with excessive tearing of the left eye and mild ocular pain. Over the previous 4â€“5 months he had noticed progressive left-eyelid drooping and intermittent diplopia. He reported a mild left-sided head trauma 1.5 years earlier but had no other medical history or family history of neurological or autoimmune disease.

On ophthalmologic examination, visual acuity, intraocular pressure, and funduscopy were normal. The left eyelid was ptotic, and there was limitation of adduction, elevation, and depression of the left eye, with a dilated left pupil. No other neurological deficits were found. Fluorescein angiography, indocyanine green chorioangiography, and optical coherence tomography were unremarkable. The patient noted that his ptosis fluctuated during the day, with marked improvement when washing his face with cold water.

To evaluate for myasthenia gravis, an ice pack was applied to the left eyelid for 2 minutes, after which his ptosis improved significantly, with near-complete eyelid opening; the improvement lasted only a few minutes.
case.diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Myasthenia gravis was considered because of fluctuating ptosis and cold-induced improvement â€” â€œInterestingly, the patient reported fluctuation of his left-eyelid droop during daytime and significant improvement when washing his face with cold water.â€ 2. Pupillary involvement argued against MG since ocular MG does not affect pupils â€” â€œocular MG is characterized by diplopia and/or ptosis, whereas pupillary function is always normal.â€

case.title: Mycoplasma pneumoniaeâ€associated encephalitis complicated by cerebral salt wasting syndrome
case.case_prompt:
An 8-year-old girl was brought to the emergency department with reduced consciousness and an 8-day history of high fever, accompanied by vomiting, epigastric pain, and a mild cough. On arrival, her pulse was 112/min, respiratory rate 19/min, blood pressure 93/62 mm Hg, and temperature 36 Â°C. Physical examination was unremarkable. Neurologically, she was drowsy and disoriented (GCS 14/15), with impaired memory, visual hallucinations, and an unsteady gait; deep-tendon reflexes were normal, and plantar responses were flexor. Initial blood tests showed acute respiratory acidosis and mildly elevated liver enzymes; serum sodium was 135 mEq/L, BUN 11 mg/dL, and creatinine 0.49 mg/dL. Chest radiography revealed a slight increase in bilateral lung markings. Brain CT was normal. 

In the pediatric ICU, lumbar puncture showed CSF with protein 26 mg/dL, glucose 82 mg/dL, and 5 lymphocytes/Î¼L. Twelve hours after admission, she developed complex partial seizures with secondary generalization and was started on phenytoin and acyclovir. By day 3, seizures increased in frequency with auditory and visual hallucinations; MRI was normal. Serologies were negative for HSV I/II, CMV, and EBV IgM, but Mycoplasma pneumoniae IgM was positive (3.44), and a 5-day course of azithromycin was begun. Blood and CSF cultures remained sterile. EEG showed diffuse slowing, rhythmic parietalâ€“temporal discharges, and left parietal spikes. By day 4, seizures progressed to generalized myoclonic type despite levetiracetam, phenytoin, oxcarbazepine, and valproate, and her consciousness declined to GCS E2V2M5. 

On day 6, she developed marked polyuria: intake 1324 mL versus output 2606 mL, with signs of dehydration (decreased skin turgor, tachycardia, 0.6 kg weight loss). Laboratory evaluation revealed serum sodium 112 mmol/L, serum osmolality 227 mOsm/kg, urine sodium 129 mmol/L, urine osmolality 437 mOsm/kg, and a fractional excretion of sodium of 2.76Ã—10â»Â².
case.diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion was considered because hyponatremia frequently accompanies central nervous system disorders, but it was excluded due to evidence of hypovolemia and polyuria â€” â€œThe definitive characteristic that distinguishes CSW from SIADH is volume status.â€ 2. Cerebral salt wasting syndrome was identified as the cause because the patient met established criteria of hypotonic hyponatremia, polyuria, elevated urine sodium, and volume depletion â€” â€œIn this case report, the patient was diagnosed as having CSWS because of the following findings: (i) polyuria was noted during the clinical course; (ii) the patient had hypotonic hyponatremia; (iii) hypovolemia was diagnosed based on the signs of dehydration; and (iv) renal loss of sodium and a negative sodium balance were noted.â€

case.title: Bilateral retinal vasculitis associated with cold agglutinin disease treated with obinutuzumab and infliximab
case.journal: American Journal of Ophthalmology Case Reports
case.case_prompt:
A 69-year-old Hispanic woman was referred for progressively worsening blurry vision, right eye greater than left. Her past ocular history included bilateral epiretinal membranes. Medical history was notable for nonspecific joint disease managed with low-dose prednisone and methotrexate, and pulmonary symptoms of unclear etiology for which she was also on low-dose prednisone. 

On examination, best-corrected visual acuity was 20/40 in the right eye and 20/25 in the left. Intraocular pressures were 13 mm Hg OU. Slit-lamp examination showed nuclear sclerotic cataracts OU (2+ OD, 1+ OS) without cell or flare. Dilated fundus examination revealed 0.5+ vitreous haze OU and mild vessel attenuation OU. Spectral-domain OCT showed preserved foveal contour without sub- or intraretinal fluid. Wide-angle fluorescein angiography demonstrated mild bilateral peripheral perivascular leakage.

Initial laboratory tests including liver function, basic metabolic panel, hemoglobin A1c, angiotensin-converting enzyme, lysozyme, QuantiFERON, syphilis serologies, antiâ€“dsDNA, and chest radiography were within normal limits, except for a positive antinuclear antibody titer of 1:80. Complete blood count showed leukocytosis (11.6 K/ÂµL) and anemia (hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL; previously 10.2 g/dL one year earlier).

Methotrexate was discontinued; the patient was started on mycophenolate mofetil 1000 mg twice daily and prednisone 20 mg daily. Two months later, visual acuity remained stable (20/25 OD, 20/20 OS), but repeat fluorescein angiography showed worsening peripheral perivascular leakage.
case.diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Infectious etiologies were considered given the broad causes of retinal vasculitis â€” â€œThe differential diagnosis for retinal vasculitis is wide-ranging and includes infectious causes (e.g. bacterial, viral, fungal, parasitic),â€ but none were identified â€” â€œNo etiology was found on systemic examination and extensive evaluations for infectious uveitis.â€ 2. Ocularâ€specific inflammatory disorders were considered (eg, Ealeâ€™s disease, birdshot chorioretinopathy, pars planitis) â€” â€œThe differential diagnosis for retinal vasculitis is wide-ranging and includes â€¦ ocular disorders (e.g. Ealeâ€™s disease, birdshot chorioretinopathy, pars planitis, acute multifocal hemorrhagic retinal vasculitis, Vogtâ€“Koyanagiâ€“Harada syndrome, etc.),â€ but exam lacked characteristic anterior or posterior segment inflammation â€” â€œSlit-lamp examination was remarkable only for nuclear sclerotic cataracts â€¦ with no cell or flare in OU.â€

case.title: Epsteinâ€“Barr virus-related dacryocystitis: a case report
case.case_prompt:
A 6-year-old girl was admitted with a 5-day history of fever (to 39.5 Â°C) and a 2-week history of unilateral clear tearing from the right eye. She had been treated elsewhere for presumed bacterial conjunctivitis with topical ofloxacin without improvement. Over the previous 4â€“6 weeks she had chronic nasal obstruction, noisy breathing, and rhinolalia, and for 7 days she had purulent anterior rhinorrhea.  
On examination, she had erythematous, tender, and edematous right upper and lower eyelids with an S-shaped palpebral swelling and overlying erythema at the site of the lacrimal sac. There was painful palpation at the nasal canthus but no ocular discharge. Nasal mucosae were swollen. Bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy was present, more marked on the right. Neurologic examination, ocular motility, and funduscopy were normal, with visual acuity 20/20 bilaterally and no proptosis or relative afferent pupillary defect. Throat and abdominal examinations were unremarkable.  
Laboratory studies showed a normal white-cell count with a left shift, C-reactive protein 22 mg/L, and normal procalcitonin.  
Contrast CT of the head revealed right preseptal soft-tissue infiltration and a well-delimited ipsilateral 11 Ã— 18 Ã— 13 mm collection with rim enhancement suggestive of a periorbital abscess; there were no signs of acute sinusitis or intracranial extension. Multiple bilateral cervical lymph nodes were also noted.  
Intravenous co-amoxiclav (100 mg/kg/day) and topical neomycinâ€“polymyxin B were initiated. Over the next 3 days, fever persisted, and a fluctuating lump developed in the nasolacrimal region. Systemic corticosteroids were added. No spontaneous purulent discharge occurred, and blood cultures plus eye-swab cultures remained negative. Surgical drainage was considered, and EBV serology was sent.
case.diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Bacterial periorbital abscess was considered because imaging showed â€œa well-delimited collection â€¦ with enhanced borders suggesting a periorbital abscess.â€ 2. Acute sinusitis was excluded since the CT scan â€œdid not reveal any signs of acute sinusitis or intracranial complications.â€

case.title: DRESS Syndrome due to Nevirapine Treated with Methylprednisolone
case.case_prompt:
A 47-year-old woman with HIV-1 infection presented with a 10-day history of diffuse maculopapular rash, fever, myalgias, arthralgias, choluria, pruritus, and right upper-quadrant abdominal pain. Three days before admission she developed nausea, vomiting, and decreased oral intake. Her HIV had been well controlled on zidovudine, lamivudine, and lopinavirâ€“ritonavir for the past three years, with CD4 counts consistently >500 cells/mm3. Forty-five days before presentation her regimen was changed to zidovudine, lamivudine, and nevirapine because of gastrointestinal intolerance. Twenty days after starting nevirapine the rash and jaundice began. 

On examination: temperature 39.0Â°C; blood pressure 95/66 mm Hg; marked conjunctival and skin icterus; diffuse maculopapular rash covering >50% of body surface; no lymphadenopathy; abdomen soft with tenderness in the right hypochondrium; no hepatosplenomegaly; neurologic examination normal.

Laboratory studies showed: normal complete blood count and differential; normal glucose, electrolytes, and pancreatic enzymes; elevated aspartate aminotransferase, alanine aminotransferase, bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase, and Î³-glutamyl transpeptidase; acute kidney injury with a threefold increase in serum creatinine and a 75% decrease in estimated glomerular filtration rate. Testing was negative for cytomegalovirus antigenemia, hepatitis A IgM, hepatitis B and C antibodies. An electrocardiogram was normal. Abdominal ultrasonography showed a liver of normal size and shape with homogeneously increased echogenicity of the parenchyma.
case.diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Acute viral hepatitis was considered but excluded â€” â€œTesting was negative for cytomegalovirus (CMV) antigenemia, antibodies to HBV and HCV, and IgM antibodies to hepatitis A virus.â€ 2. Primary CMV infection was ruled out â€” â€œTesting was negative for cytomegalovirus (CMV) antigenemiaâ€¦â€ 3. Drug hypersensitivity was suspected given timing with nevirapine initiation â€” â€œshe was admitted to our hospital twenty days after initiating the new regimen because of a diffuse maculopapular rash accompanied by discrete yellow discoloration of the whites of the eyesâ€¦â€

case.title: Panic Attack, Severe Hypophosphatemia and Rhabdomyolysis in the Setting of a Motor Functional Neurological Disorder
case.case_prompt:
A 22-year-old athletic man with no other medical history presented with two episodes of acute tetraparesis.

First episode (military service): During intense physical exercise and a stringent diet, he developed hyperventilation, hypocapnia, severe hypophosphatemia without rhabdomyolysis, and acute tetraparesis. Cerebral and spinal MRI findings were unremarkable. Workup for myasthenia gravis was negative, with normal nerve conduction studies and no acetylcholine receptor antibodies. Hypokalemic periodic paralysis was ruled out by short/long exercise tests and genetic analysis of SCN4A, KCNJ18, KCNJ2, and CACNA1S. He underwent a 4-week in-patient rehabilitation program and fully recovered within 6 months.

Second episode (one year later): He presented to the emergency department with a panic attack manifesting as acute hyperventilation and anxiety, accompanied by paresthesia and tetany in all four limbs. It was unclear whether the sensorimotor symptoms preceded or followed the panic attack. He reported normal physical activity and a balanced diet in the preceding months.

On examination, he had tetraparesis with muscle strength of 4/5 (Medical Research Council scale), a positive Trousseauâ€™s sign on the right hand, normal cranial nerves, no sensory deficit, and normal deep tendon and plantar reflexes. Gait and balance were not assessed in the acute setting.

Arterial blood gas analysis showed acute respiratory alkalosis: pH 7.57 (NR 7.35â€“7.45), PaCOâ‚‚ 19.2 mmHg (NR 30â€“45 mmHg), PaOâ‚‚ 105 mmHg (NR 82.5â€“105 mmHg), HCOâ‚ƒâ» 17.7 mmol/L (NR 22â€“26 mmol/L), and a base excess of â€“1.3 mmol/L (NR Â±2â€“3 mmol/L). Blood chemistry revealed severe hypophosphatemia (0.2 mmol/L) and rhabdomyolysis (creatine kinase 44 913 U/L; AST 397 U/L; ALT 133 U/L). Other blood tests, including sodium, potassium, magnesium, calcium, vitamins B1, B2, B6, B9, B12, amino acids, and TSH, were normal. Urinalysis showed no renal phosphate loss. Nerve conduction studies and myography were normal.
case.diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Myasthenia gravis was excluded â€” â€œworkup for myasthenia gravis was negative (normal nerve conduction studies and absence of acetylcholine receptor antibodies).â€ 2. A structural central nervous system lesion was excluded â€” â€œcerebral and spinal cord MRI results were unremarkable.â€

case.title: Primary perivascular epithelioid cell tumor of the liver: new case report and literature review
case.case_prompt:
A 63-year-old woman presented with a 1-year history of atypical right upper-quadrant abdominal pain, significant fatigue, and weight loss. Her medical history was unremarkable, with no cirrhosis, viral hepatitis, or tuberous sclerosis complex. On examination she had mild hepatomegaly without palpable mass or jaundice. Liver function tests and tumor markers (AFP, CEA) were within normal limits.  
Abdominal ultrasonography revealed a solitary, heterogeneous, hypoechoic mass in the liver with an ill-defined margin; color Doppler imaging demonstrated abundant blood flow at the lesionâ€™s periphery. Contrast-enhanced CT showed a 6.4 Ã— 8.0 cm low-density mass in segment IV with inhomogeneous enhancement in the portal phase. MRI confirmed a lesion that was hypointense on T1-weighted images, hyperintense on T2-weighted images, and showed strong heterogeneous enhancement after gadolinium administration. There was no lymphadenopathy, portal vein involvement, or other lesions.  
Given the hypervascular nature of the mass, its lack of adipose tissue, and absence of thick-walled vessels, the lesion was initially interpreted as a hemangioma, an atypical hepatocellular carcinoma, or a metastatic focus. Because of the patientâ€™s worsening symptoms and indeterminate imaging findings, a central segmentectomy of the liver with cholecystectomy was performed.
case.diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Hemangioma was considered because of the hypervascular imaging appearance â€” â€œThese radiologic findings of hypervascular lesion can be confused, most often, with those of hepatocellular carcinoma or simple haemangioma.â€ 2. Hepatocellular carcinoma was considered for the same hypervascular features â€” â€œThese radiologic findings of hypervascular lesion can be confused, most often, with those of hepatocellular carcinoma or simple haemangioma.â€ 3. Metastatic lesion was included in the preoperative differential based on imaging and lesion characteristics â€” â€œGiven the hypervascular character, the lack of adipose tissue and thick-walled blood vessels, the lesion has been interpreted as hemangiomas, atypical hepatocellular carcinoma or metastatic lesion.â€

case.title: Myofibrillar Myopathy Mimicking Polyneuropathy
case.case_prompt:
A 76-year-old man presented with a 5-year history of progressive leg weakness. He reported right foot slap and tripping on uneven ground, later developing bilateral foot drop, exercise-related leg fatigue, and mild difficulty climbing stairs or rising from a low chair. There was no bulbar or upper-limb involvement. He also described sensory loss in both feet to mid-shin level with mild superficial dysesthesia. A prior workup suggested bilateral L5 radiculopathy, but lumbar MRI was normal.

On examination, there was no obvious muscle atrophy. Manual strength testing revealed isolated distal lower-limb weakness: ankle dorsiflexion 2/5 on the right, 4â€“/5 on the left; inversion and eversion 4/5; plantar flexion difficulty when standing on toes. Hip girdle and thigh strength were 5/5. Pinprick and light-touch sensation were reduced in a length-dependent distribution; vibration and proprioception were intact. Reflexes were 1+ in the upper limbs and absent at the knees and ankles.

Laboratory studies showed normal CBC, vitamin B12, TSH, serum protein electrophoresis, hemoglobin A1c, paraneoplastic antibodies, and renal and liver function tests. Creatine kinase was mildly elevated at 508 U/L. A 30-gene Charcotâ€“Marieâ€“Tooth panel was negative. Because EMG showed myotonic potentials, genetic testing for myotonic dystrophy types 1 and 2 was performed and was negative.

Nerve conduction studies revealed mildly reduced peroneal and tibial CMAP amplitudes with normal distal latencies and conduction velocities. Sensory nerve conduction studies were within normal limits. Needle EMG demonstrated fibrillations, positive sharp waves, and polyphasic, long-duration motor-unit potentials with reduced recruitment in the tibialis anterior (most prominent) and gastrocnemius. Prominent myotonic discharges were also noted in several muscles. The biceps brachii and vastus lateralis were normal. Overall, these findings were interpreted as consistent with a predominantly motor length-dependent polyneuropathy.
case.diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Acquired length-dependent polyneuropathy â€” â€œThese results overall were in keeping with a predominantly motor length-dependent polyneuropathy.â€ 2. Small-fiber sensory neuropathy â€” â€œThe normal sensory conduction studies, in the context of distal sensory symptoms and impaired pain and touch sensation on examination, suggested a restricted involvement of small/unmyelinated sensory fibers.â€ 3. Myotonic channelopathies and dystrophic myotonias â€” â€œMyotonic discharges â€¦ are more typical of channelopathies and dystrophic myotonias.â€ 4. Inflammatory myopathies and acid maltase deficiency â€” â€œThey have been described in a wide range of other myopathies, notably the inflammatory myopathies and acid maltase deficiency.â€ 5. Myofibrillar myopathy â€” â€œThey may also occur to be a characteristic feature of myofibrillar myopathies.â€

case.title: Primary renal leiomyosarcoma: A diagnostic challenge
case.case_prompt: A 62â€yearâ€old woman presented with angular stomatitis and intermittent mild-to-moderate abdominal pain for 6 months. She was found to be severely anemic (hemoglobin, 6 g/dL) but denied hematuria, postmenopausal bleeding, or gastrointestinal bleeding; occult blood in stool and red cells in urine were negative. Physical examination was otherwise unremarkable. Renal ultrasonography demonstrated left hydronephrosis; all other abdominal organs appeared normal. Intravenous pyelogram showed an opacity in the left renal region exerting mass effect on the pelviâ€calyceal system. Contrastâ€enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed a 9.0 Ã— 7.5 cm solid, enhancing softâ€tissue mass with focal necrosis, inseparable from the medial left kidney, displacing the kidney laterally and compressing the collecting system. At exploratory laparotomy, a radical nephrectomy specimen measured 17 Ã— 12 Ã— 8 cm. The cut surface showed a wellâ€circumscribed, lobulated, firm, grayâ€white mass (10 Ã— 9 Ã— 8 cm) in the lower pole, with a whorled appearance, focal necrosis, and myxoid degeneration, displacing the pelvis. Microscopically, the tumor was composed of interlacing fascicles and whorls of spindle cells with moderate eosinophilic cytoplasm, oval-toâ€cigarâ€shaped nuclei with moderate pleomorphism, 20â€“25 mitoses per 10 highâ€power fields, and approximately 20% of the tumor showing hemorrhage and necrosis; no epithelial component or perinephric infiltration was identified. Immunohistochemical analysis showed diffuse strong positivity for smooth muscle actin, desmin, and vimentin; epithelial membrane antigen was focally positive, while pancytokeratin and highâ€molecularâ€weight cytokeratin were negative.
case.diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Retroperitoneal sarcoma was considered on imaging because of a large softâ€tissue mass inseparable from the kidney â€” â€œA possibility of retroperitoneal sarcoma was considered on the CT scan.â€ 2. Leiomyoma was excluded because the tumor exhibited high mitotic activity and necrosis, features seen only in malignant smooth muscle tumors â€” â€œmitoses and necrosis are present only in malignant tumor, though cellular pleomorphism can be seen in both.â€ 3. Sarcomatoid renal cell carcinoma was excluded by the lack of cytokeratin expression and the presence of desmin positivity, along with classic smooth muscle fascicles â€” â€œCytokeratin positivity with absence of myoid markers supports a diagnosis of sarcomatoid renal cell carcinoma â€¦ presence of desmin is diagnostically helpful since this is positive in leiomyosarcoma and not in sarcomatoid carcinoma.â€

case.title: Complete horizontal gaze palsy due to bilateral paramedian pontine reticular formation involvement as a presentation of multiple sclerosis: a case report
case.case_prompt: A 16-year-old Iranian girl presented with sudden-onset bilateral horizontal gaze palsy that developed overnight. She reported paresthesia in both feet beginning 2 months earlier, progressing over 2 weeks to her trunk at the level of the umbilicus, and a similar episode in both hands about 1 year prior. She had no significant past medical history, took no medications, and denied illicit drug, tobacco, or alcohol use. On examination, she had complete bilateral horizontal gaze palsy in the horizontal plane while vertical gaze was intact aside from slightly decreased saccadic velocity. Pupils were midsize and reactive; there was no nystagmus, diplopia, or blurred vision. Mild bilateral peripheral facial nerve palsy was present. Hyperreflexia was noted in all four limbs without clonus; plantar reflexes were downgoing; muscle strength, tone, and coordination were normal. Brain MRI showed multiple hyperintense subcortical and periventricular lesions on FLAIR and T2-weighted images, and bilateral hyperintensities in the paramedian pontine reticular formation (PPRF) at the level of the pons. Spinal MRI revealed two hyperintense lesions, one at C7 and another adjacent to T4. After gadolinium, the PPRF lesions did not enhance, whereas the T4 lesion enhanced. Visual evoked potentials were normal. Serum antiâ€“aquaporin-4 and antiâ€“myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein antibodies were negative. All other laboratory tests and routine studies were unremarkable.
case.diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Bilateral abducens nucleus lesions would produce similar gaze palsy, but lesions were localized to the PPRF â€” â€œlesions affecting the abducens nuclei will cause ipsilateral horizontal gaze palsy ... bilateral horizontal gaze palsy will be caused if lesion(s) involve both abducens nuclei.â€ 2. MLF lesions (internuclear ophthalmoplegia) were unlikely because they preserve abduction and impair adduction, whereas this patient had complete horizontal gaze loss â€” â€œisolated damage to the MLFs will cause the classic INO where eye abduction is preserved, however, the ipsilateral eye will not be able to be adducted on conjugate horizontal gaze.â€ 3. Bilateral PPRF lesions account for the clinical findings, as the PPRF connects frontal eye fields to the abducens nuclei and its involvement leads to horizontal gaze palsy â€” â€œLesions involving the PPRF bilaterally will also lead to bilateral horizontal gaze palsy similar to the involvement of bilateral abducens nuclei, as PPRF acts as a relay center that connects the frontal eye fields in the cerebral cortex to the abducens nuclei.â€ 4. Infarction or vasculitis were unlikely given imaging features consistent with demyelination and unremarkable laboratory studies â€” â€œThis presentation can also have various other etiologies such as infarct, vasculitis, and NMO.â€

case.title: Neurolymphomatosis of the brachial plexus from atypical primary central nervous system lymphoma lesions: A case report and review of the literature
case.case_prompt:
A 70-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation on enoxaparin, liver cirrhosis, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and a history of venous thrombus presented with a 5-day history of left arm weakness, numbness in the left fourth and fifth digits and medial palmar surface, and confusion. She reported chronic vision loss in the right eye but denied headache or trauma. On examination, she was alert; oriented to person, place, and time; and disoriented to situation. Visual acuity was reduced in the right eye. Muscle strength was 4/5 in the left triceps, wrist flexors, wrist extensors, and finger flexors, and 3/5 in the left finger extensors. The left triceps reflex was absent. Sensation to pinprick and light touch was decreased in the left fourth and fifth digits. There was no dysmetria or dysdiadokokinesia. Head CT without contrast showed a 1 cm hyperdense round lesion in the suprasellar cistern.

On hospital day 2, she developed a pupil-sparing right third cranial nerve palsy, which progressed the next day to a dilated, nonreactive right pupil. Gadolinium-enhanced MRI of the brachial plexus and spine was unrevealing. Gadolinium-enhanced brain MRI revealed enhancing lesions with heterogeneous signal intensity in the suprasellar, pineal, and right periatrial regions, without evidence of acute ischemic stroke. One week later, repeat brain MRI demonstrated interval enlargement of these lesions and focal right frontoparietal dural thickening. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast showed multiple thyroid nodules concerning for metastases. Two serial lumbar punctures yielded lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, and an elevated IgG index but no malignant cells or other inflammatory or infectious markers.
case.diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Embolic stroke was considered given her atrial fibrillation and diabetes but felt less likely â€” â€œWhile the patient had several risk factors for embolic stroke, including atrial fibrillation and diabetes, the specific patterns of sensory loss in the left upper extremity and loss of the left triceps reflex were more suggestive of either nerve root or brachial plexus pathology.â€ 2. Extradural or intradural extramedullary spinal canal mass and idiopathic brachial neuritis were considered given her focal arm findings â€” â€œOur differential diagnosis included a lateralized mass in the extradural or intradural extramedullary spinal canal or idiopathic brachial neuritis.â€ 3. Leptomeningeal metastases from carcinoma or lymphoma were considered after CN III involvement â€” â€œConsiderations included leptomeningeal disease from metastatic carcinoma or lymphoma as well as PCNSL...â€ 4. Primary central nervous system lymphoma was considered due to her age and multifocal neurologic presentation â€” â€œConsiderations included...PCNSL due to the patientâ€™s age and multifocal nature of her symptoms...â€ 5. Tuberculosis, histoplasmosis, coccidioidomycosis, and cryptococcosis were considered among infectious etiologies â€” â€œConsiderations included...tuberculosis, histoplasmosis, coccidioidomycosis, or cryptococcosis...â€ 6. Sarcoidosis was considered among inflammatory causes â€” â€œConsiderations included...sarcoidosis.â€ 7. Neurolymphomatosis of the brachial plexus was considered but not supported by imaging â€” â€œGadolinium-enhanced brachial plexus and spine MRI were unrevealing.â€ 8. Metastatic thyroid carcinoma was considered given the CT findings â€” â€œChest/ abdomen/pelvis CT with contrast demonstrated multiple nodules in the thyroid concerning for metastases.â€ 9. Leptomeningeal dissemination and infection were less likely after nondiagnostic CSF studies â€” â€œCSF studies from two serial lumbar punctures...demonstrated lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, and elevated IgG index but neither malignant cells nor other inflammatory and infectious markers.â€

case.title: Liver Microabscesses in a Premature Neonate With Eosinophilic Colitis
case.case_prompt:
A 33 4/7-week, 2.22-kg female neonate was born after spontaneous vaginal delivery following premature rupture of membranes. She had an unremarkable physical examination at birth and passed meconium within 24 hours. Maternal breast milk feeds were initiated by gavage on day 1 of life. 

On day 4, after episodes of bilious emesis, she was made nil per os. Examination showed a distended, tender abdomen without rebound and normoactive bowel sounds; the remainder of her exam, including genitourinary assessment, was normal. Laboratory studies on day 4 revealed a white-cell count of 21.1Ã—10^9/L with 14% eosinophils. 

By day 5, she developed frank bloody stools. Serial abdominal radiographs showed nonspecific bowel gas fullness without pneumatosis. An upper gastrointestinal series ruled out malrotation. Although abdominal distention resolved by day 6, leukocytosis, eosinophilia, and hematochezia persisted. Family history was notable for a sister with milk-protein intolerance who developed hematochezia after milk exposure. 

Broad-spectrum antibiotics (ampicillin and cefepime) were started on day 5. On day 7, ampicillin was replaced with vancomycin after an abdominal ultrasound was performed to evaluate for ischemia, intussusception, and colitis; the ultrasound showed several subcentimeter hypoechoic, hypovascular liver lesions characterized as microabscesses, scattered loops of thick-walled bowel with minimal ascites, a sludge-filled gallbladder, and no biliary ductal dilatation. There was no pneumatosis, pneumoperitoneum, portal venous gas, or complex ascites. 

On day 8, a contrast-enhanced CT scan confirmed irregular hypodensities in the anterior right hepatic lobe consistent with microabscesses and demonstrated mild mucosal hyperenhancement and bowel-wall thickening of the rectosigmoid.
case.diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Necrotizing enterocolitis â€” â€œNo pneumatosis, pneumoperitoneum, portal venous gas, or complex ascites made NEC unlikely.â€ 2. Intussusception â€” â€œAbdominal ultrasoundâ€¦to evaluate for ischemia, intussusception, and colitis, which showed several subcentimeter hypoechoic, hypovascular liver lesions in the anterior liver characterized as microabscesses.â€ 3. Colonic ischemia â€” â€œAbdominal ultrasoundâ€¦to evaluate for ischemiaâ€¦which showedâ€¦scattered loops of thick-walled bowel with minimal ascitesâ€

case.title: Thyroid follicular renal cell carcinoma excluding thyroid metastases: A case report
case.case_prompt: A 60-year-old man presented with a 1-month history of right lower back and abdominal pain. He had a 10-year history of poorly controlled diabetes. Physical examination was unremarkable. Laboratory tests showed CA 19-9 of 99.98 Î¼mol/mL, creatinine 73.5 Î¼mol/L, and split glomerular filtration rates of 34.9 mL/min bilaterally. Renal ultrasound revealed a 0.9 Ã— 0.8 cm round hypoechoic mass in the mid-to-lower parenchyma of the right kidney with no internal blood flow. case.title: Congenital subglottic cyst: a rare cause for sudden airway compromise in a newborn
case.case_prompt:
A male infant was born at 39 weeksâ€™ gestation via non-elective cesarean delivery for failed induction of labor in the setting of intrauterine growth restriction. Birth weight was 2730 g (<10th percentile) and Apgar scores were 8 and 8. Within the first hour of life the infant developed progressively worsening biphasic stridor, respiratory distress, and hypoxemia with oxygen saturations falling to 60%. He was started on nasal continuous positive airway pressure at 6 cm Hâ‚‚O with FiOâ‚‚ 0.21 and a nasogastric tube was placed. Despite this support, there was continued respiratory compromise, and he was transferred to a tertiary pediatric center.

On arrival, the respiratory rate was 70 breaths/min with marked intercostal recession and tracheal tug; he had a weak cry. A venous blood gas revealed a pH of 7.10 and a PaCOâ‚‚ of 75 mm Hg despite escalation of respiratory support. Fibreoptic nasendoscopy performed at the bedside demonstrated a normal supraglottic anatomy and vocal cords but revealed a large subglottic mass causing nearâ€“complete occlusion of the airway.

case.title: Clear Cell Adenocarcinoma of the Renal Pelvis in a Male Patient
case.case_prompt:
A 54-year-old man presented with right flank pain of eight monthsâ€™ duration. On examination, a palpable mass was noted in the right upper abdominal quadrant. Contrast-enhanced CT showed an enlarged right kidney (20Ã—15 cm) with hydronephrosis, nodular thickening of the pelvic lining, and enlarged hilar lymph nodes; multiple renal stones were also present. The findings were suggestive of urothelial carcinoma with lymph-node metastasis. There was no history of other neoplastic disease or prior hormonal treatment. The patient underwent right nephroureterectomy; residual matted lymph nodes with aortic encasement were left in situ. 

On gross examination, the kidney was cystically dilated, and the pelvicalyceal surface was almost entirely replaced by pale-tan papillary and solid nodules up to 2.5 cm in diameter, with only a thin rim of renal parenchyma remaining. The ureter was uninvolved. 

Microscopically, the tumor showed complex papillary structures lined by neoplastic cells with clear cytoplasm, large round nuclei with distinct nucleoli, and frequent hobnail morphology. Papillary cores contained variable stroma with hyaline basement membraneâ€“like material, and psammoma bodies were present focally. The tumor invaded into atrophic renal parenchyma and peripelvic fat. The entire pelvicalyceal surface was lined by these clear and hobnail cells. No invasive or in situ component of conventional urothelial carcinoma or nonâ€“clear cell adenocarcinoma was identified, and no benign MÃ¼llerian-type epithelium was seen. 

Immunohistochemical studies showed tumor-cell positivity for cytokeratin 7, HNF-1Î², and PAX8, with focal CA-125 expression; stains for RCC marker antigen, CD10, uroplakin III, p63, and CK20 were negative.
case.diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Conventional urothelial carcinoma was considered but excluded because â€œNo invasive or in situ component of conventional urothelial carcinoma or adenocarcinoma of non-CCA type was identifiedâ€¦â€ 2. Metastatic MÃ¼llerian clear cell adenocarcinoma was excluded by the lack of any other neoplastic lesion and the patientâ€™s sex: â€œNo clinical evidence or a previous history of any other neoplastic lesion was identified.â€

case.title: Acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM) after SARS- CoV-2 vaccination: A case reportâ˜†
case.case_prompt:
A 37-year-old previously healthy man presented with four days of progressive weakness of all four limbs, dysphagia, drooling, nausea, and vomiting. One month earlier, he had received the first dose of an inactivated SARS-CoV-2 vaccine. In the days after vaccination he developed lethargy and intermittent myalgia with partial improvement. He had no recent infection, no known COVID-19 exposures, and a negative SARS-CoV-2 PCR. 

On examination, temperature was 37.0 Â°C, heart rate 88 bpm, blood pressure 115/80 mm Hg, and respiratory rate 20 /min. He was alert and oriented. Cranial-nerve examination showed bilateral facial paralysis; pupils were 3 mm and reactive bilaterally. There was no sensory level. Muscle strength was 2/5 in all four limbs, reflexes were preserved, and plantar responses were extensor. 

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 14.1 g/dL, WBC 11,600/ÂµL, platelets 305,000/mm3, PT 17.5 s, PTT 37.2 s, INR 1.27, ESR 23 mm/hr, negative CRP, and normal chemistry. Blood and urine cultures were negative. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis showed 2 WBCs, 32 RBCs, protein 56 mg/dL, glucose 97 mg/dL (serum glucose 159 mg/dL), and no oligoclonal bands. Chest radiography and ECG were unremarkable.

On hospital day 2, brain MRI revealed poorly marginated T2-FLAIR hyperintense foci in the left cerebral peduncle, bilateral pons, and medulla with slight T1 hypointensity and faint contrast enhancement. Diffusion-weighted imaging showed restricted diffusion in the pons. MR spectroscopy showed elevated choline and myoinositol peaks. Cervical spine MRI was normal.
case.diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Guillainâ€“BarrÃ© syndrome was considered because of acute quadriparesis, but excluded because â€œThe preserved reflexes, absence of sensory deficits, and brain MRI abnormalities were against the diagnosis of GBS.â€ 2. Multiple sclerosis was considered given multifocal CNS lesions, but deemed unlikely because â€œThe lack of oligoclonal bands of CSF, bilateral neurologic signs, and configuration of lesions in MRI were not in line with the diagnosis of MS.â€ 3. Autoimmune encephalitis was considered, but excluded because â€œAutoimmune encephalitis has some key symptoms such as seizures, memory deficits, and psychiatric manifestations, none of which existed in this case.â€ 4. Cerebrovascular stroke was considered, but ruled out because â€œLack of changes in the level of consciousness, gradual progression of symptoms, and clinical and imaging localization didn't match stroke.â€ 5. Acute disseminated encephalomyelitis was supported by the post-vaccination prodrome, multifocal poorly marginated demyelinating lesions on MRI, elevated CSF protein without oligoclonal bands, and MR spectroscopy findings.

case.title: Wernickeâ€“Korsakoff syndrome in a patient with self-neglect associated with severe depression
case.case_prompt: A 68-year-old man with high-grade lymphoma diagnosed four months earlier presented with confusion. He had lost 13 kg in the month before diagnosis and, following diagnosis, developed severe depression with anorexia, losing a further 23 kg (BMI 14.5) over four months. He received chemotherapy from April to August and had a partial response. Three weeks after his last cycle, he was admitted with a three-day history of confusion; his capillary glucose was 1.9 mmol/L, and he received intravenous dextrose until normoglycaemia. He had no history of alcohol use. Initial labs showed WBC 11.9Ã—10^9/L (neutrophils 10.9Ã—10^9/L); chest X-ray, urine studies, blood cultures, and stool cultures were unremarkable. Head CT showed no acute intracranial abnormality. Over the next four days, his confusion resolved with IV dextrose, nutritional supplements, and antibiotics. On hospital day 6, he acutely worsened with new-onset confusion accompanied by ataxia, agitation, and visual hallucinations. Over the following three days, he became withdrawn and drowsy, developed episodes of hypothermia, and remained normoglycaemic; repeat infectious work-up was negative. Laboratory studies at that time showed serum magnesium 0.34 mmol/L and serum folate 3.5 ng/mL.

case.title: Simultaneous Bilateral Spontaneous Pneumothorax Revealed Birt-Hogg-DubÃ¨ Syndrome
case.case_prompt: A 43-year-old man with no prior lung disease presented to the emergency department with sudden-onset left-sided chest pain and progressive shortness of breath. He was afebrile, mildly tachycardic, and normotensive. On examination, breath sounds were decreased bilaterally and pulse oximetry showed an SpO2 of 90% on room air. Supplemental oxygen was started, and chest CT revealed a simultaneous bilateral pneumothorax that was larger on the left side. In addition, there were numerous elliptically shaped lung cysts of various sizes distributed bilaterally, predominantly in the basilar medial, basilar lateral, and subpleural regions. Bilateral chest tubes were placed, resulting in full reexpansion of both lungs with no persistent air leak after 48 hours. Radiographic findings suggested an underlying multiple cystic lung disease. Family history was notable for a maternal aunt with bullous emphysema and two episodes of pneumothorax and a deceased grandfather who had an unspecified pulmonary disease.
case.diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Emphysematous blebs and bullae â€” â€œdiffering from the typical apical location seen in cases of emphysematous blebs and bullae.â€ 2. Lymphangioleiomyomatosis (LAM) â€” â€œpatients with LAM are almost exclusively women, and their lung cysts are more diffusely distributed in both upper and lower lobes; they present a rounder shape and often a bigger size.â€ 3. Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH) â€” â€œa combination of both nodules and cysts is usually present, with the latter being often irregularly shaped and predominantly distributed in the upper- and mid-lung zones.â€ 4. Lymphoid interstitial pneumonia (LIP) â€” â€œlung cysts are associated with ground-glass opacities, nodules, and septal thickening, and a distinctive inheritance of the disorder cannot be found.â€ 5. Birt-Hogg-DubÃ¨ syndrome â€” â€œGiven all these distinguishing findings, we suspected a BHD syndrome.â€

case.title: A Novel SCNN1A Variation in a Patient with Autosomal-recessive Pseudohypoaldosteronism Type 1
case.journal: Journal of Clinical Research in Pediatric Endocrinology
case.case_prompt:
A 41-day-old female infant was referred for evaluation of recurrent electrolyte disturbances. She was born at 37 weeksâ€™ gestation to consanguineous parents by uncomplicated vaginal delivery (birth weight 3 kg). At 6 days of age, she developed poor feeding, lethargy, and frequent vomiting. In the emergency department, she was severely dehydrated with an arterial pH of 7.20, serum bicarbonate 11 mmol/L, serum potassium 10 mmol/L, and serum sodium 119 mmol/L. Cardiopulmonary resuscitation was required, and she was intubated. Initial hormonal studies were obtained, and she received hydrocortisone (stress dose), a fluid bolus, sodium bicarbonate, and measures to lower potassium. She was admitted to the neonatal ICU, tested negative for sepsis and metabolic disorders, and was discharged on hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone without sodium supplementation.  

At 20 days of age, she was readmitted with recurrent vomiting, poor feeding, and lethargy, again with hyperkalemia and hyponatremia. Treatment included hydrocortisone, fluid resuscitation, sodium bicarbonate, potassiumâ€lowering measures, and emergency peritoneal dialysis.  

Upon transfer at 41 days, she was alert, well hydrated, with normal female genitalia and no dysmorphism. Vital signs were stable. Laboratory studies showed sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 5 mmol/L, and bicarbonate 23 mmol/L; urinalysis and urine culture were normal. Fluorescence in situ hybridization was negative for SRY, and karyotype was 46,XX. Abdominal and pelvic ultrasonography were normal. Repeat hormonal studies were inconsistent with congenital adrenal hyperplasia, and she did not respond adequately to corticosteroids, requiring ongoing mineralocorticoid therapy.

Secondary PHA (transient aldosterone resistance from UTI) was considered â€” â€œincluding secondary PHA, which is a transient form of aldosterone resistance secondary to a urinary tract infection.â€ 2. Urinary tract malformation was considered â€” â€œA urinary tract malformation was also a possibility, but was unlikely because the results of the urine tests and ultrasound were normal.â€

case.case_prompt:
A 3-year-old girl presented with gradual-onset abdominal pain and non-bilious vomiting over 2 days. She was irritable but active; vital signs were stable (heart rate 98 beats/min, respiratory rate 22 breaths/min, blood pressure 90/65 mmHg, oxygen saturation 99% on room air); she was afebrile. The abdomen was soft, non-tender, with normal bowel sounds and no signs of obstruction. There was no history of fever, diarrhea, dysuria, trauma, or prior similar episodes. Family history was negative for bleeding or gastrointestinal disorders.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 7.3 g/dL (11.5â€“17.5), leukocyte count 16.1Ã—10^9/L (5.0â€“15.0), MCV 72.4 fL (76â€“96), RBC count 3.69Ã—10^6/Î¼L (4â€“6), platelets 150Ã—10^9/L (150â€“400), prothrombin time 12.7 s (11â€“15), activated partial thromboplastin time 27 s (25â€“40), and bleeding time 2 min (1â€“10). Liver and renal function tests were within normal limits except for an elevated AST of 337 U/L and CRP 43 U/L. Factor XIII activity and von Willebrand factor antigen levels were pending.

The pediatric surgical team performed an ultrasound, which showed no intussusception but revealed â€œa rounded hypo to iso-echoic lobulated area adjacent to the porta hepatis and head of the pancreas, devoid of vascularity.â€ A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the abdomen demonstrated a large intramural/submucosal duodenal hematoma along its entire length, causing luminal narrowing.
